sharedBrief:
You are advising the HAVEN/CellProtocol project on a security-sensitive authorization design. Answer in English. Do not flatter the design; disagree wherever the reasoning warrants it. State uncertainty explicitly.

CONTEXT — THE SYSTEM
HAVEN/CellProtocol is a local-first, permissioned data protocol. State lives in typed 'Cells'. Every mutation is a replayable flow event; the system guarantees deterministic, replayable behavior. Authority is domain-scoped identity: there is no global user/account authority. CellProtocol already has a real, signature-backed agreement layer: an `Agreement` is signed by an `Identity` (`Agreement.sign(identity:)`), carries `Grant`s (a `Grant` is a keypath + Permission primitive, e.g. read/write/execute on one keypath), and `Condition`s; a `CellResolver` enforces all contracts (checks identity validity, capability permissions, verifies conditions, rejects calls lacking permission, handles automatic revocation on expiry/condition-failure/identity-revocation). Book 04 security properties: no authority without explicit Contract; identity-bound and domain-scoped; deterministic and replayable; transparent and auditable.

CONTEXT — CO-PILOT CHAT AND THE ESCALATION CEILING
Co-Pilot Chat is the central GUI. When a user types a draft, a local-rules interpreter maps it to structured intent. A 'model ladder' exists (local rules -> on-device -> scoped RAG -> external API -> agent bridge). The product wants an interpreter ceiling: how high on the ladder interpretation may automatically escalate WITHOUT a per-message click. The stated V1 default is 'automatic within grant'. Six binding shipping conditions are already agreed and, except for the grant issuer, already implemented and tested as a DECISION-ONLY engine (it decides but performs no external call):
1. Interpretation only — automatic applies to lane selection for interpretation; all side effects still require a click.
2. Explicit automatic semantics in the grant — a grant to use an external model ON CLICK is NOT a grant to use it AUTOMATICALLY; escalation_mode is bound to provider kind, model class, cell scope, purpose (draft interpretation), data classes, duration, quota.
3. A deterministic LOCAL pre-flight PII/data-class gate runs on every draft before any automatic escalation; flagged content downgrades that message to ask-this-time regardless of grant.
4. Layered clamping, downward only — effective ceiling = most restrictive of {Entity base config, scaffold layer, Perspective, active grant}; layers can restrict, never widen; conference scope clamps to ask-each-time.
5. Audit before automation — every routing decision emits model.route.* flow events (recommended/invoked/denied/completed/failed) with a shared correlationId; no decision is silent.
6. Cost/quota policy with a visible meter plus a per-conversation kill switch; exhaustion or pause forces downgrade to ask-this-time.
RED LINES (non-negotiable): no hidden remote model call; no blanket AI consent; no private keys/secrets/tokens in flow payloads; interpretation output is a PROPOSAL that never enters cell state without a click.

CONTEXT — THE GAP AND THE OWNER'S DECISION
The decision engine today consumes an escalation grant that is a plain, UNSIGNED, ISSUER-LESS data struct (it has an id and a duration but no issuer, no signature, no persistence, no identity binding — any code could fabricate one). This is safe only because no live external-call path exists yet. Before wiring an actual external model call, the grant needs a REAL ISSUER. The project owner has already decided the issuer is ONLY THE USER THEMSELVES, self-signing a grant scoped to their own Entity — there is no third-party issuer, no admin, no organization, no delegation chain. Consequence to reckon with: the grant therefore does not protect against the user (who can always widen their own ceiling); it is a consent record + bound policy + revocable kill lever that protects against the SOFTWARE escalating without a deliberate, signed, revocable, audited act of the user, and against a malicious or buggy cell/process fabricating a grant.

QUESTIONS — answer each, numbered, decisively.
Q1. ARCHITECTURE FORK (A vs B). Option A: make the escalation grant a first-class CellProtocol `Agreement` with `Grant`s over specific keypaths plus a new `Condition` type encoding escalation_mode/provider/model-class/data-classes/quota/cost; inherits signature, identity-binding, domain-scoping, automatic revocation, replay-determinism, resolver enforcement for free, but requires changing high-guardrail core agreement/condition machinery. Option B: keep the rich escalation-grant descriptor as a domain policy object, but make it signed by the user's Identity, stored owner-scoped in their Entity, with issuance and revocation being resolver-gated writes on the chat cell, and the engine verifying the signature before trusting it — no core change, but a two-layer trust model. Which should HAVEN adopt for a PRODUCTION-READY system, and why? If B, state exactly which security properties of A must be re-established by hand and how. If A, state the minimum viable Condition contract and whether it is worth the core-change risk.
Q2. GRANT OWNERSHIP LAYER. Should the grant be owned at the Entity-base layer (applies across all of the user's chat scaffolds) or as a per-scaffold instance (a separate grant per scaffold, e.g. personal co-pilot vs conference)? Analyze blast radius, the downward-only clamp interaction with conference scope, revocation ergonomics, and the user's mental model. Recommend a default.
Q3. GRANT DURATION. Session-only vs persistent-with-expiry, and if persistent, the default expiry. Weigh consent freshness and the risk of silent re-escalation weeks later against consent fatigue and friction. Recommend a concrete default duration and expiry/renewal behavior, and say what must happen on expiry (hard downgrade to ask, silent renewal never).
Q4. SELF-ISSUED THREAT MODEL. Given the issuer is the user's own identity self-signing a self-scoped grant, what does the signature ACTUALLY protect against, and is self-signing meaningful or security theater? Identify which forgery/replay/confused-deputy protections remain load-bearing (e.g. a compromised cell fabricating a descriptor, replaying an expired grant, or widening scope) and the minimum mechanisms that make the self-issued grant trustworthy.
Q5. REPLAY/DETERMINISM RECONCILIATION. A grant that authorizes a NON-deterministic, cost-bearing external model call sits in tension with Book 04's deterministic+replayable guarantee, and with 'no side effect without a click' (an automatic external call IS a cost-bearing network side effect the grant pre-authorizes). How should HAVEN reconcile this? Specify what must be recorded as flow, what must stay proposal-only, how the external output is kept replay-neutral, and how quota is decremented deterministically.
Q6. PRODUCTION HARDENING. Specify the minimal but genuinely production-ready issuer design: the issuance consent surface (what the user must see about what leaves scope, model class, quota/cost cap, duration before signing), one-click revocation, quota accounting, provider-credential handling (never in the grant or flow), failure behavior (provider down -> downgrade to ask, never silent fail), and the audit chain that ties every automatic call to a specific signed grant id issued by the user at a time. What is the smallest robust build?
Q7. VERDICT. One decisive paragraph: your recommendation on Q1/Q2/Q3, and whether to split the work into sub-slice A (real issuer + signature + storage + revocation + quota accounting, still NO live external call, fully testable) followed by sub-slice B (wire exactly one real external lane behind the verified grant). Go or no-go on that split.

FORMAT
Numbered answers Q1-Q7. Maximum ~1400 words total. Cite established security/authorization concepts where relevant (capability security, macaroons/caveats, OAuth scopes, POLA, confused deputy, replay nonces) by name. Be concrete enough that an engineer could build from your answer. Disagree with the owner's framing wherever warranted.

panelists.name: GPT-5.5
panelists.roleInstructions: Prioritize correctness about capability security and authorization: what a signed self-issued grant can and cannot guarantee, principle of least authority, caveat/attenuation models (macaroons), scope binding, confused-deputy avoidance, revocation semantics. Be precise about which of Option A's inherited properties are genuinely load-bearing versus ceremonial. Give a firm A-vs-B recommendation.

panelists.name: GLM-5.2
panelists.roleInstructions: Evaluate from outside the project's assumptions. Challenge whether the two-layer descriptor model (B) is sound or whether it re-implements agreement machinery badly, and whether the owner's self-issuer decision is the right frame at all. Focus on the ownership-layer and duration decisions with clear trade-off reasoning.

panelists.name: Gemini-3.1-Pro
panelists.roleInstructions: Focus on Q5 and the audit chain: reconciling a non-deterministic cost-bearing external call with deterministic+replayable guarantees, what belongs in flow vs proposal-only state, replay-neutral handling of external output, deterministic quota accounting, and a traceable call->grant audit chain. Also assess the ownership-layer clamp interaction.

panelists.name: DeepSeek-V4-Pro
panelists.roleInstructions: Attack the design. Find the failure modes: fabricating or replaying a self-signed grant, silent re-escalation after expiry, scope-widening across scaffolds, confused-deputy via a malicious cell, cost/quota gaming, consent-surface dark patterns, and cross-runtime signature-verification gaps between web (Porthole) and native (Binding). Steelman only what survives attack; be specific about the exploit and the fix.

panelists.name: Kimi-K2.6
panelists.roleInstructions: Ground the answer in shippable production practice: the issuance consent surface and what it must disclose, one-click revocation, expiry/renewal ergonomics, provider-credential handling and rotation, failure downgrade behavior, cross-runtime parity, and whether the sub-slice A/B split is the right delivery shape. Say clearly what the smallest robust production build is.
